methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $60M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $529M - $11.0B.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series B), post-money $5B

private.name: Positron AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series B), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Atom Computing is a Series B semiconductor company founded by Ben Bloom and Jonathan King, having raised $60M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of 11 peers—3 public (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private—across the Chips & Semiconductors sector, including contemporaries like Etched, Positron AI, SambaNova Systems, d-Matrix, and DEEPX.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-Progression analysis uses the $60M total raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical for Series B advancement, yielding an implied valuation of $114M. Peer-Median analysis across the 5-company comparable set—spanning Series A through Series C and valuations from $529M to $11.0B—produces a median valuation of $2.0B. The wide peer range ($529M to $11.0B) reflects significant dispersion within frontier-semiconductor valuations.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $97M (low) to $2.3B (high), with a median of $1.1B. The two-method approach reveals material tension: stage-progression anchors conservatively near recent raise economics, while peer-median reflects current market pricing for advanced-chip companies at similar development stages. Key uncertainties include the appropriate comparability of the peer set—given heterogeneous technology focus and customer concentration—and whether recent higher-valuation comps (Etched at $5.0B, SambaNova at $11.0B) represent sustainable pricing or sector froth. Additional clarity on Atom's revenue traction, IP defensibility, and customer pipeline would narrow the range meaningfully.
